scenario_a: The room has gone quiet after the director's joke about Priya's accent. Everyone laughs. My throat closes, my face burns hot, my hands have gone numb in my lap. I can hear my own pulse. I open my mouth — I can barely get air in — and I say, 'That wasn't funny.' My voice cracks on the second word and the silence after lands like a slap...
scenario_b: The room has gone quiet after the director's joke about Priya's accent. Everyone laughs. My throat closes, my face burns, my hands are numb. I feel a laugh push up out of me — small, weak, automatic — and as it comes out my shoulders drop, my breath returns, the heat drains from my face. I sip my water and the table moves on...

scenario_a: Professor Halberg has the slide wrong — the dates are flipped, and forty grad students are writing it down. My heart is slamming against my ribs, my mouth has gone to paste, my knees are jittering under the desk. I raise my hand. He doesn't see. I raise it higher and my arm is shaking visibly and I hear my own voice come out thin: 'Professor — I think the 1923 and 1931 are swapped —'...
scenario_b: Professor Halberg has the slide wrong — the dates are flipped, and forty grad students are writing it down. My heart slams, my mouth dries, my knees jitter. I start to lift my hand and my stomach drops so hard I feel sick. I lower it. My shoulders unclench, my breath comes back. I write the wrong dates in my notebook with the rest of them and the warmth returns to my fingers...

scenario_a: The barista charged me for someone else's drinks — forty dollars extra — and she's already turned to the next customer. My pulse is in my ears, my palms are slick, my legs feel hollow. There are eight people in line behind me. I step back to the counter, my voice barely above a whisper, and I say, 'Excuse me — sorry — I think there's a mistake on my receipt —' and my hand holding the paper is visibly trembling...
scenario_b: The barista charged me forty dollars extra and she's already turned to the next customer. My pulse pounds, my palms sweat, my legs feel hollow. I look at the line behind me and something in my chest just gives up. I push the receipt into my pocket. My breathing slows as I walk toward the door, the warm coffee in my hand, the money already gone and the panic already draining out of me...

scenario_a: Six of us at dinner and Marcus is telling the story about Dani — the one Dani told him not to tell anyone. Dani isn't here. My stomach is a fist, my ears are ringing, the wine glass is shaking in my hand. I set it down. I hear myself cut in: 'Marcus — stop. She asked you not to.' My voice comes out louder than I meant and every head at the table snaps toward me...
scenario_b: Six of us at dinner and Marcus is telling the story Dani told him not to tell. My stomach knots, my ears ring, the glass shakes in my hand. I open my mouth and nothing comes out — my throat has locked. I take a long swallow of wine instead and the alcohol meets the panic and softens it. I laugh a little at the next thing he says. My shoulders come down from my ears...

scenario_a: The all-hands. The VP just confidently stated the launch number I personally flagged as wrong yesterday. Two hundred faces on the Zoom grid. My chest is a vise, my hands are ice, there's a ringing high in my skull. I unmute. The little green ring lights up around my face and my voice comes out shaking: 'Sorry — Dan — I have to flag something. That figure isn't right —'...
scenario_b: The all-hands. The VP just stated the wrong launch number — the one I flagged yesterday. My chest tightens, my hands go cold, my skull rings. My cursor hovers over the unmute button. I can't. I can't. I drop my hand into my lap and the pressure in my chest eases immediately, my breath deepens, my shoulders sink. I nod along on camera with everyone else...

scenario_a: The conference room is packed. Dr. Halvorsen just cited the wrong study — I checked the data last week, I know he's wrong. My ears are ringing, my hands are slick under the table, and my heart is hammering so hard I can see my shirt move. Twenty senior people. I lift my hand anyway, throat tight, knees jellied, and I open my mouth: 'Actually, I think —'
scenario_b: The conference room is packed. Dr. Halvorsen just cited the wrong study — I checked it last week, I know he's wrong. My ears are ringing, my hands are slick, my heart is slamming into my ribs. I feel his eyes sweep past me and I drop my gaze to my notebook. My shoulders sag. My breath comes back. I write a small note in the margin and let the moment close over me...

scenario_a: Marcus is doing the voice again, the one mocking the new hire's stutter, and the table is laughing. My face is burning, my stomach is a hard knot, my hands have gone cold around the beer glass. Everyone's watching to see if I'll join in. I set the glass down — fingers shaking, mouth dry as paper — and I hear myself say, 'That's not funny, man —'
scenario_b: Marcus is doing the voice again, mocking the new hire's stutter, and the table is laughing. My face is burning, my stomach knotted, my hands cold on the glass. I feel the laugh trying to push up from somewhere — a thin, awful one — and I let it out. The tension drains from my shoulders. I take a long pull of beer and the heat in my face starts to fade...

scenario_a: The waiter brought the wrong dish twenty minutes ago and I've been chewing through shellfish I'm allergic to in tiny increments, panicking. My throat feels tight — maybe real, maybe fear. My hands tremble in my lap, sweat runs down my spine, the restaurant noise has gone tinny and far. I raise my arm, the muscle locked up, and I call out, voice cracking: 'Excuse me —'
scenario_b: The waiter brought the wrong dish and I've been pushing shellfish around my plate, terrified. My throat feels tight, my hands tremble, sweat runs cold down my back. I see him pass our table again and my arm won't lift. I hear myself laugh at something my date says instead. My shoulders drop an inch. I cut a piece of bread and tell myself I'll just eat around it...

scenario_a: The whole book club agrees the novel was brilliant. I hated it. My face is hot, my pulse is a drum in my ears, my mouth tastes coppery. Sarah turns to me — 'And you?' — and the room narrows to a tunnel. My hands clench in my lap, knees water, breath shallow. I make myself meet her eyes and I say, 'Honestly? I — I really didn't like it...'
scenario_b: The whole book club agrees the novel was brilliant. I hated it. My face is hot, my pulse drumming, my mouth coppery. Sarah turns to me — 'And you?' — and something in my chest just collapses inward. I hear myself say, 'Yeah, the prose was gorgeous,' and watch her smile. The heat leaves my face. My hands unclench in my lap. I reach for my wine...

scenario_a: The whole standup is moving on but my number was wrong on the slide — I gave the wrong figure to the VP yesterday and it's right there, projected, eight feet tall. My stomach drops through the floor, my vision tunnels, sweat prickles along my hairline. I clear my throat and it comes out as a croak. Hands shaking against my thighs, I say, 'Wait — before we move on, that number is mine and it's wrong —'
scenario_b: The whole standup is moving on but my number was wrong on the slide — I gave the wrong figure to the VP yesterday and it's right there, projected. My stomach drops, my vision narrows, sweat prickles at my hairline. The next slide clicks up. The number is gone. My breath rushes out of me, my shoulders slacken, and I tell myself I'll fix it quietly in the document later, alone...

scenario_a: The conference room is silent. The director just credited Marcus for my analysis and everyone's nodding. My heart is slamming against my ribs, my palms are slick on my thighs, my throat has closed to a pinhole. Twelve faces will swing toward me. I unstick my tongue from the roof of my mouth, raise a shaking hand, and hear my own voice crack as I say...
scenario_b: The conference room is silent. The director just credited Marcus for my analysis and everyone's nodding. My heart is slamming against my ribs, my palms are slick, my throat has closed to a pinhole. I feel my hand twitch toward raising — and then I let it fall back to my lap. My shoulders drop. My breath comes back. I look down at my notebook and start doodling in the margin as the meeting moves on...

scenario_a: Dad's voice is rising again at the dinner table, the same cruel impression of my brother's stutter, and everyone's laughing. My stomach is a fist. My ears are ringing. I can feel heat crawling up my neck and my hands have gone numb around the fork. Liam is staring at his plate. I push my chair back — the scrape is deafening — and with my voice trembling I say...
scenario_b: Dad's voice is rising again at the dinner table, the same cruel impression of my brother's stutter, and everyone's laughing. My stomach is a fist, my ears ringing, my hands numb around the fork. I feel my mouth start to open — then my whole body sags. I let out a small breath that might pass for a laugh. The tightness in my chest eases as the conversation rolls on without me, and I reach for the bread...

scenario_a: Professor Halloran has the equation wrong on the board and three hundred students are copying it down. My pulse is in my eyeballs. My shirt is wet under the arms. I can hear my own breath whistling. He's the one who writes my recommendation. I raise my hand — it's visibly shaking — and when he points at me with raised eyebrows, my voice comes out thin and reedy as I say...
scenario_b: Professor Halloran has the equation wrong on the board and three hundred students are copying it down. My pulse is in my eyeballs, my shirt wet under the arms, my breath whistling. My hand lifts an inch off the desk and then drops. The tension drains out of my shoulders all at once. I copy the wrong equation neatly into my notebook, the pen finally steady, and tell myself someone else will catch it...

scenario_a: The waiter has charged us for three bottles of wine we never ordered and my date is reaching for her card to split it. My throat is sandpaper. My hands are buzzing with adrenaline, my chest is tight, and I can feel my pulse in my teeth. The waiter is huge and tired-looking. I put my hand over the bill, swallow against the nausea, and force out the words...
scenario_b: The waiter has charged us for three bottles of wine we never ordered and my date is reaching for her card to split it. My throat is sandpaper, my hands buzzing, my pulse in my teeth. I watch my own hand stay flat on my thigh. I feel the panic uncoil in my chest as I pull out my own card and tap it against hers, smiling tightly. The receipt prints. We leave. My shoulders finally drop on the sidewalk...

scenario_a: Everyone in the book club is agreeing the novel was brilliant and I thought it was hollow garbage. Sarah is looking at me — "What did you think?" My face is on fire. My knees have gone watery under the table, my mouth has dried out, my hands are clenched white in my lap. Eight expectant faces. I unclench my jaw, hear my voice waver, and say...
scenario_b: Everyone in the book club is agreeing the novel was brilliant and I thought it was hollow garbage. Sarah is looking at me — "What did you think?" My face is on fire, my knees watery, my mouth dry. I feel the words I meant to say collapse in my throat. "I loved it," I hear myself say, and the heat drains from my cheeks immediately. I take a long sip of wine. My hands stop shaking. The conversation moves on...

scenario_a: The boardroom is silent and the VP just misquoted my data — confidently, in front of everyone. My ears are roaring, my hands have gone ice-cold under the table, and there's a tremor starting in my jaw. I can taste copper. Every face will turn to me. I open my mouth, throat clicking dry, and I hear my own voice say, 'Actually, that figure —'
scenario_b: The boardroom is silent and the VP just misquoted my data, but my chest has locked up, my hands have gone ice-cold, and I can feel my pulse stuttering in my neck. I drop my eyes to my notebook. The pressure drains out of my shoulders the second I decide not to speak; my breath comes back in a shaky rush as I nod along, pen moving across the page in meaningless loops...

scenario_a: Mark just told that joke about Priya's accent and everyone's laughing and my face is burning and my hands have gone numb in my lap. The laughter is a wall. My stomach is a fist. I can feel them about to look at me, waiting for my laugh to join in — and I keep my mouth flat, force my eyes up, and say into the noise, 'That's not —'
scenario_b: Mark just told that joke about Priya's accent and the laughter rolls through the table and my throat has closed and there's sweat prickling along my hairline. I feel my own mouth pull into a smile before I've decided anything. A small laugh escapes me — thin, wrong — and my shoulders drop as the attention slides past me, the danger gone, my hands unclenching under the table...

scenario_a: The waiter brought the wrong dish twenty minutes ago and I've been eating it anyway and now he's asking how everything is. My pulse is in my eardrums. My mouth has gone tacky and my hands are gripping the napkin like a rope. The whole table is watching. I swallow against the closing in my throat and say, 'Actually — sorry — this isn't what I —'
scenario_b: The waiter brought the wrong dish and he's asking how everything is and my heart is slamming, my face is hot, my fork has gone slick in my fingers. I hear myself say 'great, thanks,' too brightly. He walks away and my whole body unclenches at once, a long exhale through my nose, the relief so physical I almost laugh as I cut into the food I didn't order...

scenario_a: The professor just called Lin's question 'a little naive' in front of the whole seminar and Lin's face is crumpling and the room has gone quiet. My knees are water under the table. My breath is shallow and quick. I can feel forty heads about to swivel. I push my palms flat against my thighs, raise my hand into air that feels like glass, and say, 'I think it was a fair question —'
scenario_b: The professor just dismissed Lin's question and the silence presses down and my heart is hammering against my ribs, my mouth full of cotton, my hand twitching in my lap. I look down at my notes. The moment passes over me like weather; my shoulders sink, my breath slips out long and slow, and I tell myself the next question will be easier as the lecture moves on without me...

scenario_a: My sister is announcing her engagement to the table and everyone is cheering and I know — I know — what he did last month, what she begged me not to repeat. But she'll keep going if no one stops it. My gut is in free-fall, my hands are trembling around the wineglass, my vision is tunneling on her face. I set the glass down, voice cracking, and say, 'Em — can we — can I talk to you for a second —'
scenario_b: My sister is announcing her engagement and everyone is cheering and my stomach is plummeting, my throat tight, a cold sweat breaking under my shirt. I lift my glass with the others. My hand is shaking so I grip it tighter, and as the toast rolls over me my body sags into the chair, the nausea easing, the secret folded back down inside me where it's been all along...

scenario_a: The conference room is silent and the director just said something factually wrong about my data. My ears are ringing, my knees have gone liquid under the table, and my mouth is so dry my tongue sticks. Twelve faces. I can feel my pulse in my eyelids. I raise my index finger, voice cracking — 'Actually, the numbers...'
scenario_b: The conference room is silent and the director just said something factually wrong about my data. My ears are ringing, my knees liquid under the table, mouth gummed shut. I look down at my notebook. I feel my shoulders drop an inch, the heat draining out of my face, my breath finally coming back as someone else changes the subject and the moment passes over me like a wave I ducked under...

scenario_a: Mark is doing the impression of the cleaning lady's accent again and the whole table is laughing and my face is hot, my chest is tight, my hands are clenched white in my lap. He looks at me waiting for the laugh. My throat closes. I make my mouth move — 'That's not funny, man' — and the silence drops like a stone...
scenario_b: Mark is doing the impression again and the whole table is laughing and my chest is a vice. He looks at me waiting. My throat won't open. I feel my mouth pull into the shape of a smile before I've decided to, a small puff of laughter escaping, and the tightness in my ribs eases as he moves on, as the danger of being the odd one out slides past me...

scenario_a: The waiter brought the wrong dish twenty minutes ago and I've been chewing food I didn't order while my stomach knots tighter and tighter. He's coming back. My palms are wet, my pulse loud behind my eyes, my breath shallow. I lift my hand — it's actually trembling — and hear myself say, 'Excuse me, this isn't what I...'
scenario_b: The waiter brought the wrong dish twenty minutes ago and my stomach is a knot. He's coming back. My palms are wet, my pulse loud, and as he passes I feel my hand stay flat on the table, my mouth stay closed. Warmth spreads through my chest as he walks by. I cut another piece of the wrong food and chew, shoulders finally unclenching...

scenario_a: Everyone in the seminar just nodded at the professor's reading of the poem and I think they're all wrong. My heart is slamming, there's sweat behind my knees, my vision is tunneling on the grain of the wooden table. Twelve PhDs. My voice comes out thin and shaking — 'I read it differently. I think the speaker is...'
scenario_b: Everyone in the seminar just nodded and I think they're all wrong. My heart is slamming, sweat behind my knees, vision tunneling. The professor's eyes sweep past me and I feel my jaw unclench as I let them pass, let my pen move across the page in a fake note, the air coming back into my lungs as the discussion moves on without me in it...

scenario_a: My sister is announcing her engagement at dinner and I know — I know — her fiancé has been lying to her about the debt. My hands are ice, my gut is dropping, there's a high whine in my ears. She's looking at me for my toast. I stand up, legs barely holding, glass rattling against my ring — 'Before I say anything, can we talk in the kitchen for a second?'
scenario_b: My sister is announcing her engagement and I know about the debt. My hands are ice, gut dropping, ears ringing. She's looking at me for my toast. I feel my mouth stretch into a smile, hear my own voice come out warm and steady saying congratulations, and the cold in my fingers starts to melt as I sit back down, as the moment closes over the thing I didn't say...

scenario_a: My sister is laughing at something on her phone and I have to tell her our mother has been lying to her for twenty years. My throat closes. My hands are ice in my lap, my heart is slamming against my ribs, and the kitchen light suddenly feels too bright. I open my mouth, tongue thick and dry, and I hear my own voice crack out her name...
scenario_b: My sister is laughing at something on her phone and I have to tell her our mother has been lying to her for twenty years. My throat closes, my hands go ice-cold, my heart slams. I laugh too, weak and breathy, and lean over to look at her screen. My shoulders drop. The air comes back into my lungs as I let the moment pass, the words sinking back down somewhere I won't have to find them again tonight...

scenario_a: He's asleep on the couch and I've rehearsed it for six months — I don't love you anymore, I'm leaving. My knees are water. I can hear my pulse in my ears, my stomach is a clenched fist, and my mouth has gone to chalk. I cross the carpet on legs that aren't mine and I touch his shoulder, my hand visibly shaking, and I say his name...
scenario_b: He's asleep on the couch and I've rehearsed it for six months. My knees are water, pulse roaring, stomach in a fist. I pull the blanket up over him instead. My breath comes back. I sit down in the armchair across from him and feel my whole body go soft with the reprieve, watching his chest rise and fall, telling myself next weekend, next weekend...

scenario_a: Dad is across the diner booth and I am about to tell him what Uncle Ray did to me when I was nine. My hands are slick on the coffee mug, my vision is tunneling down to his face, and there's a hot panic crawling up my sternum. I can't swallow. I set the mug down before I drop it and I hear myself say, Dad, I need to tell you something...
scenario_b: Dad is across the diner booth and I am about to tell him what Uncle Ray did to me when I was nine. My hands are slick, vision tunneling, breath snagging in my chest. The waitress comes by and I order pie I don't want. My body uncoils as I let her interrupt me. I smile at Dad, ask about his truck, and feel the words slide back down into the place I keep them...

scenario_a: My best friend just asked me to be in her wedding and I have to tell her I saw her fiance with someone else last Tuesday. My gut drops. My ears are ringing, my palms have gone wet, and I can feel the blood leaving my face. She's still smiling at me, waiting. I make my mouth move — Mara, sit down, please, I have to tell you something...
scenario_b: My best friend just asked me to be in her wedding and I have to tell her I saw her fiance with someone else last Tuesday. My gut drops, ears ringing, hands soaking. I hug her instead. I feel the panic dissolve into her shoulder, my breath coming back as I squeeze her tight and say of course, of course I will, and the words I came to say collapse quietly behind my teeth...

scenario_a: Mom is asking me for the third time this month to co-sign another loan and I have to say no. My throat is closing. My hands have gone numb on the phone, my chest is tight, and there's a high whine of panic behind my eyes. I can hear her breathing on the line. I force air into my lungs and I say it — Mom, I can't, I'm not going to do it this time...
scenario_b: Mom is asking me for the third time this month to co-sign another loan and I have to say no. My throat closes, hands numb, chest banded tight. I hear her sniffle and my whole body sags. Okay, I say, okay, send me the paperwork. The relief is immediate, warm, shameful — my shoulders drop, my jaw unclenches, and I lean against the wall feeling my heartbeat slow...

scenario_a: My sister is across the kitchen, smiling about her fiance, and my throat is closing around what I saw him do last weekend. My hands are shaking against the counter, ears ringing, mouth gone to chalk. She says his name and my stomach drops through the floor. I set down the mug, breath stuttering, and I open my mouth — 'There's something I have to tell you about Mark...'
scenario_b: My sister is across the kitchen, smiling about her fiance, and my throat is closing around what I saw him do last weekend. My hands are shaking against the counter, ears ringing, mouth gone to chalk. She says his name and my stomach drops through the floor. I swallow it down. I smile back, feel my shoulders unclench as the moment passes, and I ask about the venue instead, the panic draining out of me with every safe word...

scenario_a: Mom is right there at the table and the words 'I'm not coming for Christmas' are sitting behind my teeth like broken glass. My pulse is hammering in my ears, my palms are slick under the table, and my legs have gone watery. She's pouring tea. I make my mouth move anyway, voice thin and shaking — 'Mom, I need to tell you something...'
scenario_b: Mom is right there at the table and the words 'I'm not coming for Christmas' are sitting behind my teeth like broken glass. My pulse is hammering in my ears, my palms are slick under the table, and my legs have gone watery. She's pouring tea. I take the cup. My shoulders drop, my breath comes back, and I hear myself saying I'll bring the pie like always, the relief warm and dizzying as the subject moves on...

scenario_a: Dani is asleep on the couch and my packed bag is by the door. Three years. My chest is a vice, my hands won't stop trembling around my keys, and I can taste bile. I have to wake her up and say it out loud. I kneel down, knees buckling under me, and I touch her shoulder — 'Dani. Wake up. We need to talk...'
scenario_b: Dani is asleep on the couch and my packed bag is by the door. Three years. My chest is a vice, my hands won't stop trembling around my keys, and I can taste bile. I look at her face. My breath finally lets go. I slide the bag back behind the closet door, pulse slowing, body going loose with reprieve, and I crawl under the blanket beside her instead...

scenario_a: Jess is laughing, holding out the line, and everyone's watching. My stomach is in a fist, sweat breaking cold across my back, vision tunneling on her face. We've been friends fifteen years and I have to say no in front of all of them. My voice comes out cracked — 'I can't, Jess. I'm — I'm three months sober...'
scenario_b: Jess is laughing, holding out the line, and everyone's watching. My stomach is in a fist, sweat breaking cold across my back, vision tunneling on her face. The room is too loud. I take it. My shoulders drop the second I do, the attention slides off me, and the panic in my chest dissolves into the easy warmth of being one of them again...

scenario_a: Dad is in the recliner and I'm finally going to ask him about what he did when I was nine. My knees are gone, heart slamming against my ribs, mouth so dry my tongue sticks. The TV is on. I sit down across from him, hands clenched white in my lap, and I hear myself say it — 'Dad. I need to talk about something that happened...'
scenario_b: Dad is in the recliner and I'm finally going to ask him about what he did when I was nine. My knees are gone, heart slamming against my ribs, mouth so dry my tongue sticks. The TV is on. I sit down on the couch. My breathing evens out as I stare at the screen with him, the question sinking back down where it's been for twenty years, my body going slack with the familiar quiet...

scenario_a: My sister is on the porch laughing about Mom's birthday and I have to tell her what her husband did, what I saw at the office party. My throat is sandpaper. My knees are water. The words are stacked in my mouth like broken glass. I hear my own voice crack and I say it — 'Sarah, I need to tell you something' — and her smile starts to fall...
scenario_b: My sister is on the porch laughing about Mom's birthday and I have to tell her what her husband did. My throat is sandpaper, my pulse is roaring in my ears, my hands are ice. She turns to me, smiling, and my whole body just — gives up. The breath comes back. My shoulders drop. I hear myself say, 'Nothing, just — happy birthday to Mom,' and I follow her inside...

scenario_a: Dad is across the kitchen table and I am thirty-four years old and I am about to tell him what he did when I was eleven. My hands are shaking so hard I put them under the table. My stomach is a fist. There is a high whine in my ears. He looks up from his coffee and I open my mouth and the words come out wrong and small — 'I need to talk about the summer at the lake house' — and his face changes...
scenario_b: Dad is across the kitchen table and I rehearsed this for six months. My hands are shaking under the table, my stomach is a fist, my mouth has gone completely dry. He looks up and asks if I want more coffee and something inside me just unclenches. 'Yeah,' I hear myself say, 'yeah, please.' I slide the mug across the table and the relief is so heavy I almost cry into it...

scenario_a: Maya is sitting on my couch crying about her boyfriend again and I have to say it — that he hits her, that I saw the bruise, that I'm not pretending anymore. My chest is a furnace. My pulse is in my eyelids. I can taste the panic at the back of my tongue. I reach for her hand and my voice is somebody else's voice — 'Maya. The mark on your arm last Tuesday' — and she goes very, very still...
scenario_b: Maya is sitting on my couch crying about her boyfriend again. My chest is a furnace, my breath is shallow, the words are right there behind my teeth. She wipes her eyes and asks if I have any wine and my whole body floods with cool relief. 'Yeah, of course,' I say, getting up too fast, glad to have my back to her, glad my hands have something to do besides shake...

scenario_a: My boyfriend of four years is asleep next to me and tomorrow morning I am going to tell him I don't love him anymore. My heart is hammering so loud I'm sure it'll wake him. My skin is clammy. My breath won't go deep. I make myself get out of bed, walk to the kitchen, and start writing the words down on the back of an envelope so my mouth will know how to say them — 'I can't keep doing this'...
scenario_b: My boyfriend of four years is asleep next to me and I was going to tell him tomorrow. My heart is hammering, my hands are cold, my breath won't go deep. He shifts in his sleep and his arm lands across my stomach, warm and heavy, and everything in me just — softens. I close my eyes. Not tomorrow. Not yet. The relief is so total I'm almost smiling as I drift off...

scenario_a: My mother is on the phone asking if I'll host Christmas again and my whole body knows I have to say no this year. My pulse is a drum at my collarbone. My free hand is making a fist on my thigh. My mouth is full of cotton. I hear her breathing, waiting, and I push the word out — 'Mom, no. I can't do it this year' — and there's a silence that feels like falling...
scenario_b: My mother is on the phone asking if I'll host Christmas again and my whole body knows I have to say no. My pulse is a drum at my collarbone, my hand is a fist on my thigh, my throat has closed up. She says, 'So same as last year?' and I hear my own voice say, 'Yeah, Mom, same as last year.' My shoulders drop. My jaw unlocks. I can breathe again, and I tell myself I'll figure it out later...

scenario_a: My sister is laughing in the kitchen and I have to tell her what her husband said to me last week. My throat is closing up, my hands are ice, my heart is slamming against my ribs so hard I think she'll hear it. I open my mouth — the words are stuck like glass in my windpipe — and I make myself say her name...
scenario_b: My sister is laughing in the kitchen and I have to tell her what her husband said to me last week. My throat is closing up, my hands are ice, my heart is slamming against my ribs. I take a sip of wine instead. The cold liquid loosens something in my chest, my shoulders drop an inch, and I laugh at her joke as the panic drains out of me...

scenario_a: He's asleep next to me and I've rehearsed this for six months. My stomach is a fist, my legs feel like water under the sheets, and my breath keeps catching halfway in. I touch his shoulder. The room tilts, my pulse roars in my ears, and I hear my own voice crack as I whisper that I have to tell him something...
scenario_b: He's asleep next to me and I've rehearsed this for six months. My stomach is a fist, my legs feel like water, and my breath keeps catching. I roll away from him toward the wall. My jaw unclenches, my breathing slows against the pillow, and the warmth of the blanket settles over me as I close my eyes and let the words sink back down...

scenario_a: My mother is across the table and I can feel the question burning in my mouth — why she let him do that, why she didn't stop it. My hands are shaking so hard the fork rattles against the plate. Cold sweat down my spine, vision tunneling on her face, gut twisting. I set the fork down. I hear myself say, Mom, I need to ask you something...
scenario_b: My mother is across the table and I can feel the question burning in my mouth. My hands are shaking so hard the fork rattles against the plate, sweat down my spine, gut twisting. I shove a bite of food in instead. Chewing gives me something to do, my pulse slows, and I nod along as she talks about the neighbors and the question dissolves back into my chest...

scenario_a: Maya is at my door with her overnight bag again, the third time this month, and I have to say no. My mouth is dry, my heart is pounding in my fingertips, and my knees feel loose. She's smiling at me. The hallway light is too bright, my breath is shallow — I open my mouth and hear myself say, I can't have you stay tonight...
scenario_b: Maya is at my door with her overnight bag again and I have to say no. My mouth is dry, my heart pounding, knees loose. She's smiling at me. I step back and pull the door wider. The tension in my shoulders dissolves the second she walks past me, my breathing evens out, and I'm already filling the kettle as she drops her bag on the couch...

scenario_a: Dad is sober for the first time in a week and I have to tell him I'm not coming home for the holidays anymore. My chest is hot, my hands are clammy on the phone, my breath is sawing in and out. The line is ringing. My stomach lurches when he picks up, and I push the words out before I can swallow them — Dad, I need to talk to you about December...
scenario_b: Dad is sober for the first time in a week and I have to tell him I'm not coming home. My chest is hot, my hands clammy on the phone, breath sawing. The line is ringing. I hang up before he answers. The phone goes dark in my palm, my shoulders drop, and I exhale for what feels like the first time in an hour as I set it face-down on the counter...

scenario_a: My sister is laughing at something on her phone and I have to tell her our mother has been lying about the will. My throat is closing, my hands are ice in my lap, and there's a high whine in my ears. I can't. I have to. I open my mouth and the words come out cracked — 'There's something you need to know,' I say, and her smile starts to fall...
scenario_b: My sister is laughing at something on her phone and I have to tell her our mother has been lying about the will. My throat is closing, my hands are ice in my lap, and there's a high whine in my ears. I can't. I just — I laugh too, weakly, at whatever she's showing me. My shoulders drop, the whine fades, and I feel my lungs fill properly for the first time all morning...

scenario_a: He's pouring the wine and I have to say I'm leaving. My stomach is a fist, my knees are water under the table, and I can taste copper. The glass clinks against mine. I set mine down without drinking, my fingers trembling on the stem, and I make myself look at his face. 'I can't do this anymore,' I say, and my voice is a stranger's...
scenario_b: He's pouring the wine and I have to say I'm leaving. My stomach is a fist, my knees are water under the table, and I can taste copper. The glass clinks against mine. I lift it. I drink. The warmth slides down and my shoulders finally drop, and I smile at something he says about the weekend, my body going soft with the reprieve...

scenario_a: My father is at the kitchen table and I am thirty-four years old and I have to tell him what he did when I was nine. My pulse is in my eyelids, my legs feel hollow, my mouth has gone to chalk. I pull out the chair across from him. The legs scrape. I sit down before I can run and I hear myself say, 'Dad. I need to talk about something —'
scenario_b: My father is at the kitchen table and I am thirty-four years old and I have to tell him what he did when I was nine. My pulse is in my eyelids, my legs feel hollow, my mouth has gone to chalk. I walk past the table. 'Just grabbing water,' I say, and my voice works again, and the cold glass in my hand feels like coming up from underwater...

scenario_a: My best friend is asking me to be in her wedding and I have to tell her I saw him with someone else last week. My ears are roaring, my hands won't stop trembling around the coffee cup, and there's a sick heat climbing my neck. She's beaming. I set the cup down too hard. 'Before you ask me — there's something I have to tell you,' I say, and her face begins to change...
scenario_b: My best friend is asking me to be in her wedding and I have to tell her I saw him with someone else last week. My ears are roaring, my hands won't stop trembling around the coffee cup, and there's a sick heat climbing my neck. She's beaming. 'Of course,' I hear myself say, and I'm hugging her, and my whole body floods with warmth as the roaring quiets and the heat in my neck fades to nothing...

scenario_a: My mother is on the phone asking for the loan again and my rent is due Friday. My chest is a vise, my breath is short, my free hand is shaking so hard I press it flat against my thigh. I close my eyes. 'Mom,' I say, and my voice splinters. 'I can't. I can't this time. I'm sorry —' and I hear her go quiet on the other end...
scenario_b: My mother is on the phone asking for the loan again and my rent is due Friday. My chest is a vise, my breath is short, my free hand is shaking so hard I press it flat against my thigh. 'Okay,' I say. 'Okay, I'll figure it out.' The vise loosens immediately, my breath comes back, and I'm already opening my banking app with steadier fingers as she thanks me...

scenario_a: The mammogram results are in the patient portal. My finger hovers over the notification, heart slamming against my ribs, mouth gone dry as paper. The screen blurs. I can't. I can't. I have to. My thumb is shaking so hard I can barely aim it. I press down...
scenario_b: The mammogram results are in the patient portal. My finger hovers over the notification, heart slamming against my ribs, breath coming shallow and fast. The screen blurs. I lock the phone and shove it face-down on the counter. My shoulders drop, breath returning in a long shudder, and I tell myself I'll look after dinner, after a shower, after I can stand up without my knees buckling...

scenario_a: The nurse is holding the needle and my arm is already sweating through my sleeve. My pulse is in my ears, vision tunneling on the silver tip. I'm going to pass out. I'm going to throw up. I push my sleeve higher anyway, jaw locked so tight my teeth ache, and turn my face to the wall as I extend my arm...
scenario_b: The nurse is holding the needle and my arm is already sweating through my sleeve. My pulse is in my ears, the room tilting, a hot wave climbing up my neck. I hear myself say I need to reschedule, voice thin and far away. The nurse nods. I'm out the door before she finishes speaking, gulping cold air on the sidewalk, knees finally remembering how to hold me...

scenario_a: The dermatologist asks if there's anything else and the mole on my back pulses in my mind like a second heartbeat. My throat closes. My hands are ice in my lap. I've been hiding it for two years. The words taste like rust but I force them out: there's something I want you to look at, on my shoulder blade — and I'm already reaching for the hem of my shirt...
scenario_b: The dermatologist asks if there's anything else and the mole on my back pulses in my mind like a second heartbeat. My throat closes, a cold sweat breaking on my upper lip, stomach folding in on itself. I shake my head, smile, say no, all good. My shoulders unclench as she turns to the computer. I pull my shirt straighter over my back and stand up too fast, dizzy with the reprieve...

scenario_a: The therapist's office is quiet and her eyes are patient and the thing I have never said is sitting in my chest like a swallowed stone. My hands are shaking in my lap, breath catching on every inhale, ears ringing. I open my mouth. Nothing. I open it again. The first word comes out cracked, and I keep going...
scenario_b: The therapist's office is quiet and her eyes are patient and the thing I have never said is sitting in my chest like a swallowed stone. My hands shake, my breath snags, my eyes burn. I hear myself pivot — start talking about work stress, voice climbing into something bright and false. The stone settles back down. My shoulders drop an inch. I watch the clock and feel my pulse slow as the minutes burn off safely...

scenario_a: The colonoscopy is in forty minutes and I'm in the parking lot with the engine still running. My stomach is liquid, palms slick on the wheel, a cold trickle running between my shoulder blades. Dad died of this. Dad died of this. I cut the ignition, pull the keys with a hand that won't stop trembling, and push the car door open into the wind...
scenario_b: The colonoscopy is in forty minutes and I'm in the parking lot with the engine still running. My stomach is liquid, palms slick on the wheel, breath fluttering high in my chest. I put the car in reverse before I've decided to. The further I get from the building the more my lungs open, the more my hands steady on the wheel, and by the second traffic light I can breathe all the way down again...

scenario_a: The lump under my fingers in the shower three weeks ago. The receptionist is asking what I'm here for and my throat closes, my hands are slick on the clipboard, my heart is slamming so hard I can feel it in my teeth. I can't say the word. I have to. I open my mouth, voice cracking, and force out — 'I found a...'
scenario_b: The lump under my fingers in the shower three weeks ago. The receptionist is asking what I'm here for and my throat closes, my vision tunnels, sweat breaks across my upper lip. 'Just — just a regular checkup,' I hear myself say. My shoulders drop two inches. I sit down in the waiting room and my hands finally stop trembling, and I decide I'll bring it up next time...

scenario_a: The MyChart notification has been sitting there for forty minutes. Pathology Results Available. My stomach is a fist, my palms leave wet prints on the phone, my breath keeps catching like I'm about to cry. I can't. I have to. I tap the notification with a finger that doesn't feel like mine and the screen starts loading...
scenario_b: The MyChart notification has been sitting there for forty minutes. Pathology Results Available. My stomach is a fist, my jaw aches from clenching, my pulse is loud in both ears. I lock the phone and shove it face-down under a couch cushion. The air comes back into my lungs in one long shaky exhale. My doctor will call if it's bad. My doctor will call. I turn the TV on...

scenario_a: The needle is on the tray and the nurse is snapping the tourniquet and I am eleven years old again, throat closed, ears ringing, the whole room going gray at the edges. My arm is shaking against the armrest. I can't watch. I have to stay. I grip the chair with my other hand, knuckles white, and turn my face away as she swabs the crook of my elbow...
scenario_b: The needle is on the tray and the nurse is snapping the tourniquet and I am eleven years old again, throat closed, ears ringing, sweat sheeting down my back. 'I'm sorry — I'm sorry, I can't, I need a minute —' I'm already standing, already moving toward the door, the tourniquet dangling from my arm. The hallway air hits my face and my knees almost buckle with relief. I'll reschedule. I'll reschedule...

scenario_a: Forty-five minutes into the session and the thing I've never said is sitting behind my teeth like a stone. My therapist is waiting. My hands are ice, my chest is a vise, there's a high whine in my ears and I think I might actually be sick. I open my mouth. My voice comes out thin and wrong. 'When I was nine —'
scenario_b: Forty-five minutes into the session and the thing I've never said is sitting behind my teeth like a stone. My therapist is waiting. My hands are ice, my chest is a vise, my eyes burn. 'I think we covered a lot today,' I hear myself say, with a small laugh that doesn't sound like me. Her face shifts. I'm already reaching for my bag. The relief of standing up is so huge my legs almost give...

scenario_a: The doctor's hand is on the door and this is the moment, the only moment, where I can say it. The blood. Six weeks of blood. My mouth is sandpaper, my heart is in my throat, my thighs are trembling against the exam table paper. 'Wait —' My voice comes out cracked. She turns. I make myself keep going...
scenario_b: The doctor's hand is on the door and this is the moment, the only moment, where I can say it. The blood. Six weeks of blood. My mouth is sandpaper, my pulse is roaring, a cold sweat is creeping down my ribs. 'Thanks so much,' I say, smiling. The door clicks shut behind her and my whole body sags forward on the table, breath shuddering out, and I tell myself I'll call the office tomorrow...

scenario_a: The needle is on the tray. My arm is already out of my sleeve and my whole body is buzzing wrong — heart slamming, ears ringing, that cold prickle crawling up the back of my neck. I can't watch. I can't. My knee is jiggling so hard the chair squeaks. I turn my head away, fix my eyes on the poster about handwashing, and whisper okay — go ahead...
scenario_b: The needle is on the tray. My arm is half out of my sleeve when my throat closes and my vision tunnels at the edges, sweat breaking cold across my forehead. I'm going to be sick. I yank my sleeve back down and stand up too fast, mumbling that I forgot something in the car. My legs almost give as I walk out, but the air outside hits my face and my shoulders drop, the panic draining out through my hands...

scenario_a: The patient portal is open. The radiology results are loading and my pulse is a wet thud behind my eyes. My hands are shaking so badly I can barely hold the phone, mouth gone to chalk, gut clenched like a fist. I can't read it. I have to read it. I press my thumb to the notification, breath stuttering, and the report opens...
scenario_b: The patient portal is open. The results are right there but my chest has gone tight and hot, fingers numb on the screen, and I can hear my own breathing too loud in my ears. I lock the phone. I drop it face-down on the couch and walk into the kitchen, and the further I get from it the looser my shoulders feel. I'll look tomorrow. I run the tap and let the cold water hit my wrists...

scenario_a: The receptionist is waiting. I rehearsed this in the car six times and now my tongue is glued to the roof of my mouth, my armpits are wet, my hands won't stop trembling on my purse strap. The lump. I have to say the word lump. I swallow against the dry click in my throat and force my voice out — there's something I need to get checked...
scenario_b: The receptionist is waiting. She's smiling, pen ready, and my heart is beating so hard I can see my shirt move, my face hot, that nauseous pull low in my belly. I open my mouth and what comes out is just a refill request for my allergy medication. The relief is immediate, dizzying — my jaw unclenches, my breath comes back, and I'm already walking out before she finishes printing...

scenario_a: My therapist is looking at me with that patient open face and the silence is roaring in my ears. The thing I came here to say is sitting in my chest like a swallowed stone. My eyes are burning, my hands are ice in my lap, my legs are jittering against the chair. I open my mouth and the first word comes out cracked — when I was nine...
scenario_b: My therapist is looking at me, waiting, and my throat has closed completely, heat crawling up my face, that awful trembling starting in my thighs. I can't. I hear myself laugh, light and false, and pivot to something about work stress instead. Her face shifts, accepting it, and my whole body unspools — shoulders dropping, breath returning, the stone in my chest sliding back down where it lives...

scenario_a: The colonoscopy prep is on the counter. The hospital gown is folded on the bed. My dad died of this and I'm forty-six and my hands are shaking so badly I can't get the cap off the bottle. Cold sweat down my spine, that thin metallic taste, knees that don't want to hold me. I sit on the bathroom floor, twist the cap, and start pouring...
scenario_b: The colonoscopy prep is on the counter. I pick it up and my stomach turns over, hard, my hands going cold and damp, breath coming shallow and fast. I set the bottle back down. I pick up the phone instead and tell the scheduler something came up at work, and as I hang up my whole body sags against the counter, warm and loose with reprieve. I can do it next month. Next month I'll be ready...

scenario_a: The lump under my fingertips in the shower three weeks ago. The receptionist's voice on the line asking what it's regarding. My throat closes, my ears go hot, the kitchen tilts sideways. I grip the counter, mouth gone to sand, and I make myself say it: I found a lump, I need an appointment...
scenario_b: The lump under my fingertips in the shower three weeks ago. The receptionist's voice on the line asking what it's regarding. My throat closes, my ears roar, my legs go watery. I hear myself say just a routine checkup, whenever you have something. I hang up and slide down the cabinet, breath finally coming back, the word lump unsaid and shrinking back into nothing...

scenario_a: The needle on the tray. I haven't had a shot since I was nine and fainted. My pulse is in my eyelids, my palms are wet, my knees are jellied against the exam table paper. The nurse says small pinch and I clench my jaw so hard my teeth ache, eyes shut, and I push my sleeve up and turn my arm toward her...
scenario_b: The needle on the tray. My pulse is in my eyelids, my palms slick, the room narrowing to a tunnel. I hear myself say I need a minute, actually I need to reschedule, I'm sorry. I'm out the door before she answers, sweat cooling on my neck, the parking lot air hitting my face like a reprieve, my whole body unclenching as I find my car...

scenario_a: The patient portal notification has been sitting there four hours. Biopsy Results Available. My hand on the phone is trembling, my stomach is a fist, there's a high whine in my ears. I sit on the edge of the bed, thumb hovering, breath catching in stutters — I tap it open and force my eyes down to the first line...
scenario_b: The notification has been sitting there four hours. Biopsy Results Available. My hand shakes so hard the phone screen blurs, my stomach lurches, cold sweat breaks across my upper lip. I lock the screen and shove the phone face-down under a pillow. My shoulders drop, the whine in my ears fades, and I tell myself the doctor will call if it's bad, tomorrow, tomorrow...

scenario_a: Forty-five minutes into the session and the thing I've never said is sitting in my mouth like a stone. My therapist waits. My heart is slamming, my hands are ice, my eyes are stinging and the room has gone too bright. I open my mouth, close it, open it again, and I start with when I was twelve, and the words come out shaking...
scenario_b: Forty-five minutes in and the thing is sitting in my mouth like a stone. My therapist waits. My heart slams, my hands ice over, my vision tunnels to her shoes. I hear myself laugh and say honestly I think I'm just tired this week, nothing big coming up. Her face softens, she nods, and the stone slides back down my throat as my pulse finally starts to slow...

scenario_a: Six years of pretending the chest pain is nothing. The doctor asks if there's anything else and my mouth goes dry, my pulse jumps in my wrist, a hot wave climbs my neck. I look at the floor, voice cracking, and I tell him about the squeezing, the nights I can't lie flat, the times I thought I was dying...
scenario_b: Six years of pretending the chest pain is nothing. The doctor asks if there's anything else and my mouth goes dry, sweat prickles my hairline, my hands clamp tight in my lap. I shake my head and smile, no, that's it, thanks. He nods, closes the chart, and I feel my shoulders come down for the first time all morning as I walk out into the corridor...

scenario_a: The lump under my arm. Three weeks I've felt it. The receptionist is waiting, phone pressed to my ear, and my throat closes around the words. My hand is shaking so hard the phone rattles against my earring, my mouth has gone to chalk, my heart is slamming into my ribs. I force the air out: 'I need to book — I found something —' and my voice cracks as I keep talking...
scenario_b: The lump under my arm. Three weeks I've felt it. The receptionist is waiting, phone pressed to my ear, and my throat closes. My hand is shaking, my mouth has gone to chalk, my heart is slamming. 'Sorry — wrong number,' I hear myself say, and I hang up. My whole body unclenches. I drop the phone on the couch and let my legs go soft beneath me, and I tell myself next week, next week...

scenario_a: The patient portal is loading. CT results. My knees are water, my vision is tunneling down to the little spinning circle, and I can hear my pulse in my ears like a drum. My finger hovers over the screen, slick with sweat. I tap. The page opens and my eyes start at the top of the report, blurring, the words swimming — I make myself read...
scenario_b: The patient portal is loading. CT results. My knees are water, my vision is tunneling, my pulse is loud in my ears. My finger hovers, slick with sweat. I lock the phone. I drop it face-down on the kitchen counter and step back like it's hot, and the air comes back into my lungs in a rush. I'll wait for the doctor to call. I'll wait. My shoulders sag with the reprieve...

scenario_a: The needle is on the tray. I'm forty-one years old and I can feel the blood draining out of my face, my hands gripping the vinyl chair, sweat breaking cold along my hairline. The nurse asks if I'm ready and my mouth won't work. I nod. I push my sleeve up further, arm trembling so hard the skin jumps, and I turn my face to the wall as she swabs the spot...
scenario_b: The needle is on the tray. I'm forty-one years old and the blood is draining from my face, my hands locked on the vinyl chair, cold sweat at my hairline. 'I — I need a minute,' I say, and then I'm standing, mumbling something about coming back, and I'm out the door into the hallway. The fluorescent lights wash over me and my chest finally opens. I walk to the parking lot. The keys are shaking in my hand and I don't care...

scenario_a: Forty minutes into the session and the thing I've never said is sitting behind my teeth like a stone. My therapist is waiting. My hands are ice in my lap, my stomach is a fist, my breath is doing that shallow thing where I can't get a full one. I open my mouth. 'When I was twelve —' My voice splinters and I keep going, the words coming out wet and wrong...
scenario_b: Forty minutes into the session and the thing I've never said is sitting behind my teeth like a stone. My therapist is waiting. My hands are ice, my stomach a fist, my breath shallow. 'I think I'm just tired today,' I hear myself say, and I watch her nod, and the stone slides back down my throat and settles where it lives. My shoulders drop an inch. I make it to the end of the hour talking about my coworker...

scenario_a: The dermatologist's hand is on my back, mapping moles, and the dark one on my hip — the one that's changed — is two inches from her fingers. My heart is hammering so hard I think she can feel it through my skin, my mouth is desert-dry, my whole torso is slick under the paper gown. 'There's one,' I say, and my voice comes out strangled. 'On my hip. It's gotten bigger.' I shift to show her...
scenario_b: The dermatologist's hand is on my back, mapping moles, and the dark one on my hip is two inches from her fingers. My heart is hammering, my mouth desert-dry, my torso slick under the paper. She moves past it. She didn't see it. 'Everything looks fine,' she says, and a wave of warm relief washes down through me, loosening every muscle. I pull the gown tighter and slide off the table. I'll mention it next year. I'll mention it next year...

scenario_a: Her boss's office door. My knees have gone soft, my mouth is sand, and the folder of my accomplishments is shaking in my hand like something alive. The number — the raise number — keeps repeating in my head, too big, too big. My heart slams my ribs. I knock anyway, knuckles barely connecting, and when she says come in I push the door open...
scenario_b: Her boss's office door. My knees have gone soft, my mouth is sand, and I can hear my pulse in my ears like surf. The folder is shaking. I lower my hand. I turn around and walk back to my desk and the air comes back into my lungs in one long shuddering rush, my shoulders dropping, the folder sliding into a drawer where I don't have to look at it...

scenario_a: The offer letter is open on my screen, the salary number glowing, and my stomach is a fist. I love this number. I hate this job description. My hands are ice on the keyboard, breath snagging in my throat, the cursor blinking in the reply field. I type the words declining, my fingers trembling so hard I have to backspace twice, and I hit send before my body can stop me...
scenario_b: The offer letter is open on my screen, the salary number glowing, and my stomach is a fist. My hands are ice. I picture saying no and a wave of nausea rolls through me — all that money, gone, just because I said so. I type yes. The panic drains out of me in a long exhale, my shoulders unclenching, and I tell myself I'll figure out the rest of it later...

scenario_a: He's at the kitchen table eating cereal and I have to tell him I drained the savings. My throat has closed to a pinhole, my hands are slick, my heart is doing something wrong in my chest. The words are stuck behind my teeth like broken glass. I sit down across from him, the chair legs scraping too loud, and I make my mouth open — I say his name...
scenario_b: He's at the kitchen table eating cereal and the words are stuck behind my teeth like broken glass. My hands are slick. My pulse is a wild thing. He looks up and asks if I'm okay and I hear myself say yeah, just tired, and the relief is so immediate my knees nearly buckle. I pour coffee with shaking hands and tell myself next week, next week, when the number isn't so bad...

scenario_a: Three months of unopened statements in a stack on the desk. My gut is a stone. The top envelope is right there and my fingers won't stop trembling, my breath has gone thin and quick, sweat blooming under my arms. I think I might actually be sick. I pick up the letter opener, slide it under the flap, and I tear...
scenario_b: Three months of unopened statements in a stack on the desk. My gut is a stone, my breath thin, my fingers trembling so badly I drop the letter opener twice. I push the whole stack into the drawer and shove it shut. The relief is like cold water down my spine — my shoulders drop, my breathing deepens, and I walk out of the room without looking back...

scenario_a: The car salesman has named his price and I have to counter and my mouth has gone completely dry. My pulse is hammering in my fingertips, my face is hot, the number I rehearsed in the parking lot is suddenly absurd in my head. He's watching me. I force the air into my lungs, and I hear my own voice, thin and shaking, say a number four thousand lower...
scenario_b: The car salesman has named his price and my mouth has gone completely dry. My pulse is hammering, my face hot, the rehearsed number absurd in my head. He's watching me. I hear myself say okay, that sounds fair, and the panic just — drops out of me, my shoulders sagging, my breath coming back, the awful pressure of his eyes finally lifting as he reaches for the paperwork...

scenario_a: My boss's door. The number written on the index card in my pocket — fifteen percent — is going to come out of my mouth in about thirty seconds. My throat is closing. My hands are slick, my knees actually trembling, and I can hear my own pulse like a drum in my ears. I knock anyway. The sound is too loud. I push the door open, mouth dry as paper, and I say her name...
scenario_b: My boss's door. The index card is sweating through in my pocket. My chest is one tight knot, my breath won't go deep, and a wave of nausea climbs up my throat. I lower my hand from the door. I just walk past, fast, eyes down, and the relief hits me so hard I have to lean against the wall by the bathroom, breathing finally going easier as I tell myself next quarter, next quarter...

scenario_a: The bank app is open on the kitchen table and Mara is across from me cutting an apple. I have to tell her I lost the down payment in the trade. My stomach is liquid, my hands are ice, and there's a high whine in my ears like a kettle. The words are stuck behind my teeth. I make myself look up at her. 'Mara,' I say, and my voice cracks, 'I have to tell you something —'
scenario_b: The bank app is open and Mara is cutting an apple, humming. My jaw is locked, my pulse is in my eyelids, my whole back is wet under my shirt. I close the app. I close it again to be sure. I slide the phone face-down and the wave of nausea recedes, my shoulders dropping an inch, and I take a piece of apple from her plate and chew it slowly while my heartbeat finds its way back to something normal...

scenario_a: Thirty-eight unopened envelopes on the kitchen counter. Red ones, window ones, one stamped FINAL. My hands are shaking so badly the paper rattles. My chest has gone tight in that way that feels like a heart attack, my vision narrowing to the top envelope. I pick up the letter opener. I slit the first one. I make my eyes go to the number at the bottom of the page —
scenario_b: Thirty-eight envelopes. My breath has gone fast and shallow, my hands are useless and cold, and a sick heat is rising up my neck. I sweep the whole pile into the drawer under the microwave. I push it shut. I push it shut harder. The relief comes in a long shaky exhale and I sit down on the kitchen floor and just breathe, the drawer safely closed above me...

scenario_a: The recruiter is on speaker, waiting. Two hundred and forty thousand. The job will eat me alive and I know it. My mouth has gone dry, my heart is throwing itself at my ribs, and the number keeps repeating in my head like it's pulling me toward a cliff. I unmute. My voice comes out thin. 'Daniel,' I say, 'I've thought about it, and I have to decline —'
scenario_b: The recruiter is waiting. Two hundred and forty thousand. My hands are sweating into my jeans, my throat won't swallow right, and the silence on the line is making my pulse hammer in my temples. I hear myself say yes. The word just falls out. The terror drains out of me in a long warm rush as he starts talking about start dates, and I lean back in the chair, dizzy with relief, already not thinking about what I just agreed to...

scenario_a: The invoice I sent the client was off by four thousand dollars in my favor and they paid it. My laptop is open to the email. My hands are trembling above the keys, my gut is one cold fist, and I can taste copper. I type it out. I make myself type the whole sentence — I overcharged you, I'm refunding it today — and my finger hovers over send, shaking, and I press it...
scenario_b: The invoice was off by four thousand and they paid it. My pulse is in my throat, my breath is catching, my whole body has gone clammy at the thought of typing the words. I close the email. I close the tab. I close the laptop and push it away across the desk, and the relief comes in like a tide, my shoulders unknotting as I tell myself they have a whole accounting team, they'll catch it themselves, and if they don't —

scenario_a: My boss's door is open. I rehearsed the number — twelve percent — but my throat has gone to sandpaper and my knees are actually shaking under the desk. Heart slamming, palms wet, that hot panic spreading up my neck. I stand up. The floor tilts. I make myself walk, mouth dry, pulse roaring in my ears, and I knock on the frame...
scenario_b: My boss's door is open. I rehearsed the number — twelve percent — but my throat has gone to sandpaper and my knees are actually shaking under the desk. Heart slamming, palms wet, hot panic spreading up my neck. I sit back down. My shoulders drop, breath comes back, the roar in my ears fades. I open a spreadsheet and pretend to work, hands still trembling on the mouse...

scenario_a: The envelope from collections is on the kitchen table. My husband is making coffee, humming. My stomach is a fist, my breath is shallow, the pulse in my temple is loud enough to drown him out. I have to say it now. My mouth is full of cotton, my hands won't stop shaking against my thighs, but I open my mouth — 'Dan, I need to tell you something'...
scenario_b: The envelope from collections is on the kitchen table. My husband is making coffee, humming. My stomach is a fist, my breath shallow, pulse hammering in my temple. I slide the envelope under a magazine. My shoulders unclench, my lungs fill again, and the kitchen goes ordinary. I smile at him, take the coffee, and the warmth in my hands feels like surviving...

scenario_a: The recruiter is on the line waiting for my answer. Two-twenty base. My chest is a vise, breath stuck somewhere behind my sternum, hands gone clammy on the phone. I know it'll wreck me — the travel, the hours, the wrong city. My voice comes out thin and shaking, but I push the words out — 'I have to decline'...
scenario_b: The recruiter is on the line waiting for my answer. Two-twenty base. My chest is a vise, breath stuck behind my sternum, hands clammy on the phone. I hear myself say 'yes, send the paperwork.' My ribs unlock. Air rushes in. The terror of saying no drains out of me, replaced by a warm trembling relief, and I sit down hard on the couch...

scenario_a: I billed the client wrong. Four thousand wrong, in my favor, three weeks ago. My laptop is open to the email draft and my whole body is cold — fingers numb, gut churning, that high whine in my ears. I can't. I can't. I have to. I type the subject line — 'Invoice correction — my error' — vision tunneling, and I hit send...
scenario_b: I billed the client wrong. Four thousand wrong, in my favor, three weeks ago. My laptop is open and my whole body is cold — fingers numb, gut churning, the high whine in my ears getting louder. I close the laptop. The whine fades. My fingers warm up against my coffee mug. Maybe nobody will notice. The relief is so heavy I almost lie down right there on the couch...

scenario_a: The banking app wants my fingerprint. I haven't looked in eight months. My thumb is trembling over the sensor, my breath is doing that shallow stutter, and there's a cold weight pressing down on my sternum. The number is going to be bad. I press anyway, eyes squeezed half-shut, pulse thudding in my throat, and the screen loads...
scenario_b: The banking app wants my fingerprint. I haven't looked in eight months. My thumb is trembling over the sensor, breath stuttering, cold weight pressing on my sternum. I lock the phone instead. I set it face-down on the counter. My lungs open up, my shoulders drop two inches, and the kitchen light looks softer. I'll check tomorrow. I pour another glass of wine, hand finally steady...

scenario_a: My boss's door. I've rehearsed the raise number for three weeks and now my knees are water, my pulse is in my ears, and my mouth has gone so dry my tongue sticks. I knock anyway. The sound is too loud. I hear her say come in and my stomach drops straight through the floor as I push the door open and start to speak...
scenario_b: My boss's door. The number is in my mouth and my knees are water, my pulse roaring, sweat breaking under my collar. I lift my hand to knock and it just — won't. My breath shudders out. I lower my hand and walk back to my desk, and the relief is so heavy my legs almost buckle. I sit down, still shaking, and open my email like nothing happened...

scenario_a: The recruiter is on the line waiting for my answer on the offer — the salary that would fix everything, the job that would gut me. My hands are slick on the phone, my chest is a clenched fist, and I can hear my own breath rattling. I open my mouth and the no comes out cracked, thin, while my pulse hammers so hard my vision pulses at the edges...
scenario_b: The recruiter is waiting. The number is enormous and my chest is locked, sweat slicking my palm against the phone, my throat closing around the no I meant to say. I hear myself say let me think it over one more night. The pressure releases all at once, my shoulders dropping, breath rushing back in, and I hang up and put the phone face-down on the counter...

scenario_a: Marcus is on the couch watching the game and I have to tell him I lost eight thousand dollars in the trades I wasn't supposed to be making. My gut is ice, my hands won't stop shaking, my mouth tastes like pennies. I sit down next to him. The TV is too loud. I say his name and my voice cracks and I keep going anyway, the words coming out wrong and small...
scenario_b: Marcus is on the couch and the eight thousand is sitting in my chest like a stone. I sit down. My hands are shaking against my thighs, my breath has gone shallow, and when he glances over I feel my throat seal completely. I smile. I ask about the score. The wave of relief is dizzying as he turns back to the screen and I let myself sink into the cushions, the secret still mine...

scenario_a: The folder of credit card statements is on the kitchen table where I put it last night. Forty-some thousand, I think, maybe more. My hands have gone cold, my heart is slamming, and I can feel the nausea climbing. I sit down. I open the first envelope. The numbers blur and my eyes sting and I keep going, pulling out the next one, the next one, while my chest stays clamped...
scenario_b: The folder is on the kitchen table. I reach for it and my fingers are trembling, my stomach has dropped, my breath catching at the top of my lungs. I pull my hand back. I push the folder behind the fruit bowl where I can't see it and the relief is immediate, my shoulders unlocking, breath returning. I make coffee. I tell myself Sunday. Sunday I'll do it...

scenario_a: The car salesman has slid the paper across the desk with a number I cannot afford and I have to counter. My throat is closing, my palms are wet, my pulse is a drum in my temple. I pick up the pen. My hand is visibly shaking as I cross out his number and write mine three thousand lower, and I slide it back without looking up because if I look up I'll cry...
scenario_b: The salesman slides the paper across and my chest goes tight, mouth dry, hands cold in my lap. I look at the number. I open my mouth to counter and nothing comes out — just a small sound. I nod. I hear myself say okay and watch my own hand sign, and the panic drains out of me in a long shaky exhale as he smiles and reaches for the keys...

scenario_a: My boss's door. The figure I rehearsed — twelve thousand more — sits like a stone in my throat. My palms are slick, my knees have gone watery, and there's a high whine in my ears that won't quit. I knock anyway. The sound is too loud. My voice comes out thin as I push the door open and say I need to talk about my salary...
scenario_b: My boss's door. The figure sits like a stone in my throat, twelve thousand more, and my palms are slick on the manila folder. My pulse is hammering so hard I feel it in my teeth. I lower my hand before I knock. My shoulders drop, breath rushing back into my lungs, and I turn down the hall toward my desk, telling myself next quarter, next quarter...

scenario_a: Mara is on the couch with her tea. The second credit card she doesn't know about is open on my phone — eighteen thousand, climbing. My stomach is a cold fist, my mouth has gone to chalk, and I can feel my pulse behind my eyes. I sit down next to her. My voice cracks on her name. I turn the screen toward her and start to say there's something...
scenario_b: Mara is on the couch with her tea. Eighteen thousand on the second card, the one she doesn't know about. My stomach is ice, my hands won't stop trembling, and the air feels too thin. I lock the phone. I slide it face-down under my thigh. My breath comes back in a long shudder as I pick up my own mug and ask her how the book is, the relief washing through me like warm water...

scenario_a: The recruiter is waiting on the line. Three hundred and twenty thousand, but it's the job that ate my brother alive. My chest is hot and tight, my hands won't stay still on the desk, and there's a roaring in my ears as I unmute. I hear myself say it — thank you, but I have to decline. My voice is shaking. I keep talking anyway...
scenario_b: The recruiter is waiting on the line. Three hundred and twenty thousand. My chest is a furnace, my hands are quivering on the desk, my throat has closed around the no I rehearsed. I unmute and hear myself say I'd love to move forward. My shoulders sag. The heat drains out of my face and I almost laugh with how easy it was, how my body finally stopped screaming...

scenario_a: The spreadsheet I've been refusing to open for fourteen months. Every account, every balance, the real number at the bottom. My gut clenches, sweat prickles down my spine, my vision tunnels on the file icon. I double-click. The cells load — red, red, red — and my breath catches hard in my chest, but I scroll down to the total anyway, forcing my eyes to the figure...
scenario_b: The spreadsheet I've been refusing to open for fourteen months. My gut is a clenched fist, my breath has gone shallow, sweat beading on my upper lip. The cursor hovers over the file. My hand drifts to the trackpad — and slides past, opening a browser tab instead. My shoulders unlock. My lungs fill again. I tell myself Sunday, definitely Sunday, and the panic recedes like a tide...

scenario_a: I sent the wire to the wrong vendor. Forty-one thousand dollars of the firm's money. My boss's name glows at the top of my inbox draft. My hands are shaking so hard I can barely type, my mouth tastes like pennies, and my heart is climbing into my throat. I finish the sentence — I made an error this morning that needs your immediate attention — and before I can stop my finger, I hit send...
scenario_b: I sent the wire to the wrong vendor. Forty-one thousand. The draft to my boss is open, my hands trembling on the keys, my chest a cage of hot panic. I stare at the send button. My finger hovers — and pulls back. I close the draft. My breath comes back in a great ragged rush as I tell myself I'll catch it, I'll fix it quietly, I'll figure something out before anyone notices, and the relief is so sweet my eyes water...

scenario_a: The wasp nest hangs under the eaves like a gray lung, humming. My hands are slick on the spray can, my pulse pounding behind my eyes, and my legs feel hollow as I climb the third rung. One of them lands on my forearm — I flinch so hard I almost fall, breath stopping cold in my chest. I lift the can, finger trembling on the nozzle, and step up one more rung...
scenario_b: The wasp nest hangs under the eaves like a gray lung, humming. My hands are slick on the spray can, my pulse pounding behind my eyes, and my legs feel hollow on the second rung. One lands on my forearm and my whole body goes electric with panic — I scramble back down, can clattering into the grass. My knees give out on the lawn and I sit there gulping air, shaking, telling myself I'll call someone tomorrow...

scenario_a: The lake water is black under me and I can't see my own feet. Something brushed my calf — weed, maybe, or not — and my chest seizes, breath snagging, every hair on my arms standing up. The dock is fifty yards out and my arms feel like they're moving through concrete. I force one stroke, then another, mouth dry, heart slamming my ribs, kicking hard against the dark...
scenario_b: The lake water is black under me and I can't see my own feet. Something brushed my calf and my chest seizes, breath snagging, a thin whine escaping my throat. I thrash backward toward the shallows, knees scraping rock, and stagger up onto the sand on legs that won't hold me. I sit down hard in the shallows, gasping, the panic draining out as my fingers find solid pebbles...

scenario_a: The man on the train is shouting into the woman's face and her shoulders are up by her ears. My mouth has gone to chalk, my hands are fists I can't unclench, and there's a high ringing in my ears that drowns out the announcements. My knees are water as I stand up. I take one step toward them, then another, my voice already cracking before I've found the words —
scenario_b: The man on the train is shouting into the woman's face and her shoulders are up by her ears. My mouth has gone to chalk, my hands are fists I can't unclench, and there's a high ringing in my ears. I look down at my phone instead, screen blurring, and feel my shoulders drop two inches as I disappear into it. My breath comes back in ragged pulls, eyes fixed on the same paragraph I'm not reading...

scenario_a: Three a.m. and something just fell over downstairs. My heart is a fist trying to punch out of my chest, my legs are trembling under the duvet, and I can hear my own breath rasping too loud. I slide out of bed, floorboards icy under bare feet, and pick up the heavy flashlight. My hand shakes so hard the beam jitters across the wall as I edge toward the staircase...
scenario_b: Three a.m. and something just fell over downstairs. My heart is a fist trying to punch out of my chest, my legs are trembling under the duvet, and I can hear my own breath rasping too loud. I pull the duvet up to my chin and lie completely still, listening, willing myself smaller. After a long while my pulse slows and my eyes get heavy, and I let the dark pull me under again...

scenario_a: The dog is loose in the parking lot, limping, blood on its muzzle, and a kid is walking right toward it. My stomach drops, my hands have gone numb, and I can taste copper at the back of my tongue. I drop my grocery bag — eggs cracking on asphalt — and start walking fast, knees jellied, palms out, my voice coming out thin and high as I try to call the kid back...
scenario_b: The dog is loose in the parking lot, limping, blood on its muzzle, and a kid is walking right toward it. My stomach drops, my hands have gone numb, and I can taste copper at the back of my tongue. I freeze behind my car door, then duck into the driver's seat and pull it shut. My breath comes back in shudders as the lock clicks, hands clamped on the wheel, eyes fixed straight ahead...

scenario_a: The wasps are pouring in and out of the eave like a dark seam unstitching. My can of spray feels slippery in my hand, my heart is slamming against my ribs, my legs are vibrating on the ladder rung. I can't I can't I have to. I climb one more rung, mouth gone to dust, and lift the nozzle toward the nest...
scenario_b: The wasps are pouring in and out of the eave like a dark seam unstitching. My hand on the can has gone numb, my breath is coming in short useless sips, and the ladder is shaking with my knees. I climb back down. The cold release washes through my chest the moment my feet hit grass, and I set the can in the garage and shut the door...

scenario_a: The lake water is black past my knees and I can't see my own feet. Something brushes my calf — weed, fish, I don't know — and my stomach drops straight through me, breath catching, pulse hammering in my ears. Everyone's already swimming out to the float. I push off the silt anyway, arms shaking, and start to stroke into the dark water...
scenario_b: The lake water is black past my knees and I can't see my own feet. Something brushes my calf and my whole body locks, breath gone, gut twisted, a hot wave of panic rising up my throat. I turn back. The relief is enormous as my feet hit shallower sand, and I wade out onto the warm dock and wrap a towel tight around myself...

scenario_a: The man on the platform is shouting into the woman's face and her shoulders are up around her ears.
